title: Programming Fundamentals: Logic Building, Data Types, Loops, Functions

subtopics.title: Introduction to Programming – Algorithms and Flowcharts

blocks.title: What is Programming?
blocks.content: Programming is the process of creating a set of instructions that tell a computer how to perform a specific task. It involves problem-solving, logic building, and translating algorithms into code.

blocks.title: Algorithm – The Blueprint
blocks.content: An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. It is written in plain language and is independent of any programming language.

blocks.title: Flowchart – Visual Representation
blocks.content: A flowchart is a graphical representation of an algorithm using symbols (start/end, input/output, decision, process, connectors). It helps in visualizing the flow of logic.

blocks.title: Flowchart Symbols
rows: Indicates the beginning or end of a program.
Represents reading input or displaying output.
Shows the direction of control flow.
Connects different parts of the flowchart.

blocks.title: Steps to Solve a Programming Problem
steps: Step 2: Identify inputs, processes, and outputs.
Step 5: Write code in a programming language.
Step 6: Test, debug, and refine the code.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Programming is about solving problems using logic.

subtopics.title: Variables and Data Types – Storing Information

blocks.title: Variables and Data Types
blocks.subtitle: The Building Blocks of Data Storage

blocks.title: What is a Variable?
blocks.content: A variable is a named memory location that stores a value. It has a name, a type, and a value. The value can change during program execution.

blocks.title: What are Data Types?
blocks.content:
Data types define the type of data that can be stored in a variable. Common data types include:
• Integer (int) – whole numbers
• Floating Point (float/double) – decimal numbers
• Character (char) – single character
• Boolean (bool) – true/false
• String – sequence of characters

blocks.title: Example: Declaring Variables
given: A program needs to store a student's name, age, and average score.

blocks.title: Trap: Using the wrong data type for a value.
blocks.tip: Choose the data type that matches the nature of the data.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Data types define the kind of data stored.
Common types: int, float, char, bool, string.
Correct data types prevent errors and save memory.

blocks.title: What are Operators?
blocks.content: Operators are symbols that perform operations on operands (variables or values). Common categories: Arithmetic, Relational, Logical, Assignment, and Bitwise operators.

blocks.title: Example: Using Operators
given: int a = 10, b = 3; int c = a + b * 2;
blocks.find: What is the value of c?

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting operator precedence.
blocks.wrong: (a + b) * 2 and a + b * 2 are the same.
blocks.correct: a + b * 2 = a + (b * 2) due to precedence.
blocks.tip: Use parentheses to make precedence explicit.

blocks.title: What are Control Structures?
blocks.content: Control structures determine the flow of execution in a program. They allow the program to make decisions and execute different paths based on conditions.

blocks.title: Decision-Making Structures
rows: Executes code if condition is true.
Executes one block if true, another if false.
if (c1) { s1; } else if (c2) { s2; } else { s3; }
if (score >= 90) { grade = 'A'; } else if (score >= 80) { grade = 'B'; } else { grade = 'C'; }
switch (value) { case v1: s1; break; case v2: s2; break; default: s3; }
Selects one of many code blocks to execute.
switch (day) { case 1: print("Monday"); break; case 2: print("Tuesday"); break; default: print("Weekend"); }

blocks.title: Example: if-else Statement
given: A program checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero.

blocks.title: Example: switch Statement
given: A program prints the day of the week based on a number 1-7.

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting the 'break' statement in switch.
blocks.wrong: Without break, execution falls through to the next case.
blocks.correct: Always use break to prevent fall-through.
blocks.tip: Break is essential in switch unless fall-through is intended.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: switch: Multi-way branching based on a value.
Use break in switch to prevent fall-through.

blocks.title: What are Loops?
blocks.content: Loops are control structures that repeat a block of code multiple times. They are used when you need to perform the same task repeatedly, such as iterating over arrays or processing data.

blocks.title: Loop Types – Comparison
rows: When you know how many iterations are needed.
When you need to repeat until a condition changes.
When you need to execute at least once.

blocks.title: Example 3: do-while Loop
given: Prompt the user for input until they enter a positive number.

blocks.title: Trap: Infinite loops.
blocks.wrong: for (int i = 0; i < 10; i--) – i decreases, never reaching 10.
blocks.tip: Check the update condition to avoid infinite loops.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: for: Fixed iterations, initialization, condition, update.
while: Condition checked before execution (may run 0 times).
do-while: Condition checked after execution (runs at least once).
Avoid infinite loops by ensuring the condition becomes false.
Loops are used for repetitive tasks and data processing.

blocks.title: What are Jump Statements?
blocks.content: Jump statements alter the normal flow of program execution. They allow you to exit loops early, skip iterations, or return values from functions.

blocks.title: Jump Statements – Comparison
rows: Skips the current iteration and moves to the next.
Exits a function and returns a value.
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

blocks.title: Example: break in a Loop
given: Find the first number divisible by 7 between 1 and 100.

blocks.title: Example: continue in a Loop
given: Print all numbers from 1 to 10 except even numbers.

blocks.title: Trap: Using break in a switch without intending to exit.
blocks.tip: Break is used in both loops and switch statements.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: continue: Skips to the next iteration.
return: Exits function and returns a value.
Use jump statements to control flow efficiently.

blocks.title: Functions
blocks.subtitle: Breaking Down Code into Reusable Blocks

blocks.title: What is a Function?
blocks.content: A function is a block of code that performs a specific task. It can be called multiple times from different parts of the program. Functions promote code reusability and modularity.

blocks.title: Components of a Function
blocks.content:
1. **Return Type**: The type of value returned (void if no return).
2. **Function Name**: A unique identifier.
3. **Parameters**: Inputs to the function.
4. **Body**: The code that executes.
5. **Return Statement**: Returns a value to the caller.

blocks.title: Function Syntax and Examples
rows: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

blocks.title: Example: Writing a Function
given: Write a function that calculates the factorial of a number.
steps: for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) { fact *= i; }

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting the return statement.
blocks.wrong: int add(int a, int b) { a + b; } → no return.
blocks.correct: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
blocks.tip: A function with a non-void return type must have a return statement.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: They have a return type, name, parameters, and body.
Void functions do not return a value.
Functions should be modular and focused on a single task.

blocks.title: What is Recursion?
blocks.content: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem. Each recursive call reduces the problem size until it reaches a base case (stopping condition).

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting the base case.
blocks.wrong: int factorial(int n) { return n * factorial(n-1); } → infinite recursion.
blocks.correct: Always include a base case to stop recursion.
blocks.tip: Recursion must have a stopping condition (base case).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Every recursive function must have a base case.
Use recursion for problems that can be broken into smaller sub-problems.
Recursion can be less efficient than iteration (due to function call overhead).

blocks.title: What is an Array?
blocks.content: An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the same data type. Each element is accessed by an index (starting from 0).

blocks.title: Trap: Array index out of bounds.
blocks.wrong: arr[5] when the array size is 5 (valid indices 0-4).
blocks.correct: Always ensure index is within range.
blocks.tip: Array indices start at 0 and end at size-1.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Arrays store multiple values of the same type.
Arrays can be iterated using loops.

blocks.title: What is a String?
blocks.content: A string is a sequence of characters (letters, digits, symbols). Strings are used to store text in programs. They are typically treated as arrays of characters.

blocks.title: Example: String Concatenation
blocks.find: Combine them into a full name.

blocks.title: Trap: Strings are mutable in all languages.
blocks.correct: In Java, strings are immutable. In C/C++, strings are mutable (arrays).
blocks.tip: Know the language-specific behavior of strings.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Common operations: length, concatenation, comparison, search.
In many languages, strings are immutable (Java, Python).
In C/C++, strings are character arrays (mutable).

blocks.title: What is a Pointer?
blocks.content: A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Pointers allow direct memory access and are fundamental to C/C++ programming.

blocks.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
blocks.content: Dynamic memory allocation allows memory to be allocated at runtime. In C: malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), free(). In C++: new and delete operators.

blocks.title: Dynamic Memory Functions
rows: Allocates memory of a specified size.
Allocates and initializes memory to zero.

blocks.title: Example: Dynamic Array
given: Allocate memory for an array of 10 integers at runtime.

blocks.title: Trap: Memory leaks.
blocks.tip: Memory leaks can cause programs to crash due to exhaustion.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Dynamic memory allocation occurs at runtime.
Use malloc()/calloc() and free() in C.
Use new and delete in C++.
Always free memory to prevent leaks.

subtopics.title: Shortcuts and Memory Tricks for Programming

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – Loop Types
blocks.content: for = fixed count, while = condition-based, do-while = at least once.

blocks.title: Shortcut 2 – Data Types
blocks.content: int = whole, float/double = decimal, char = one letter, string = text, bool = true/false.

blocks.title: Shortcut 3 – Operators
blocks.content: Arithmetic (math), Relational (compare), Logical (and/or/not), Assignment (=, +=).

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Functions, Arrays, Data types – the three pillars of programming.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: Variables store data, data types define storage.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Focus on data types, loops, control structures, and functions. Understand the syntax and logic of common programming concepts.

blocks.question: Which data type is used to store decimal numbers?
blocks.explanation: float is used to store decimal numbers. int is for integers, char for characters, bool for true/false.

blocks.question: Which loop is guaranteed to execute at least once?
blocks.explanation: do-while checks the condition after executing the body, so it always executes at least once.

blocks.question: What is the output of: int a = 5; a += 3; print(a);?
Which statement is used to exit a loop early?
blocks.explanation: break is used to exit a loop early. continue skips the current iteration.

blocks.question: What is the correct syntax for an if-else statement?
blocks.explanation: The correct syntax includes parentheses around the condition and braces for blocks.

blocks.question: What is a function?
options: A block of code that performs a specific task.
blocks.explanation: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task.

blocks.question: Which operator is used for logical AND?
blocks.explanation: && is the logical AND operator. || is OR, ! is NOT.

blocks.question: What is the index of the first element in an array?
blocks.explanation: Array indices start at 0 in most programming languages (C, C++, Java, Python).

blocks.question: What is recursion?
options: A data type for storing multiple values.
blocks.explanation: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself.

blocks.question: What is the output of: int x = 10; int y = 3; int z = x / y; print(z);?
blocks.explanation: Integer division truncates the decimal part. 10 / 3 = 3 (in integer arithmetic).

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review data types, control structures (if-else, switch), loops (for, while, do-while), and functions. Practice writing simple programs to reinforce concepts.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Programming Fundamentals
points: Data types: int, float, char, bool, string.
break: Exit loop, continue: Skip iteration.
